subject: documentation_set
text: [D00003] documentation_set: SQLite is a C library that provides a lightweight disk-based database that doesn’t require a separate server process and allows accessing the database using a nonstandard variant of the SQL query language. Some applications can use SQLite for internal data storage. It’s also possible to prototype an application using S...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00004] documentation_set: The sqlite3 module was written by Gerhard Häring. It provides an SQL interface compliant with the DB-API 2.0 specification described by PEP 249 , and requires the third-party SQLite library.
title: documentation_set

text: [D00008] documentation_set: In this tutorial, you will create a database of Monty Python movies using basic sqlite3 functionality. It assumes a fundamental understanding of database concepts, including cursors and transactions .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00009] documentation_set: First, we need to create a new database and open a database connection to allow sqlite3 to work with it. Call sqlite3.connect() to create a connection to the database tutorial.db in the current working directory, implicitly creating it if it does not exist:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00010] documentation_set: In order to execute SQL statements and fetch results from SQL queries, we will need to use a database cursor. Call con.cursor() to create the Cursor :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00011] documentation_set: Now that we’ve got a database connection and a cursor, we can create a database table movie with columns for title, release year, and review score. For simplicity, we can just use column names in the table declaration – thanks to the flexible typing feature of SQLite, specifying the data types is optional. Execute the ...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00012] documentation_set: We can verify that the new table has been created by querying the sqlite_master table built-in to SQLite, which should now contain an entry for the movie table definition (see The Schema Table for details). Execute that query by calling cur.execute(...) , assign the result to res , and call res.fetchone() to fetch the ...

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00013] documentation_set: We can see that the table has been created, as the query returns a tuple containing the table’s name. If we query sqlite_master for a non-existent table spam ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00014] documentation_set: Now, add two rows of data supplied as SQL literals by executing an INSERT statement, once again by calling cur.execute(...) :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00015] documentation_set: The INSERT statement implicitly opens a transaction, which needs to be committed before changes are saved in the database (see Transaction control for details). Call con.commit() on the connection object to commit the transaction:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00016] documentation_set: We can verify that the data was inserted correctly by executing a SELECT query. Use the now-familiar cur.execute(...) to assign the result to res , and call res.fetchall() to return all resulting rows:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00018] documentation_set: Notice that ? placeholders are used to bind data to the query. Always use placeholders instead of string formatting
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00019] documentation_set: We can verify that the new rows were inserted by executing a SELECT query, this time iterating over the results of the query:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00020] documentation_set: Finally, verify that the database has been written to disk by calling con.close()
title: documentation_set

text: [D00055] documentation_set: Integer constant required by the DB-API 2.0, stating the level of thread safety the sqlite3 module supports. This attribute is set based on the default threading mode the underlying SQLite library is compiled with. The SQLite threading modes are:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00056] documentation_set: Single-thread : In this mode, all mutexes are disabled and SQLite is unsafe to use in more than a single thread at once.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00057] documentation_set: Multi-thread : In this mode, SQLite can be safely used by multiple threads provided that no single database connection is used simultaneously in two or more threads.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00058] documentation_set: Serialized : In serialized mode, SQLite can be safely used by multiple threads with no restriction.
title: documentation_set

text: [D00433] documentation_set: argparse — Parser for command-line options, arguments and subcommands — Python 3.14.4 documentation
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00434] documentation_set: While argparse is the default recommended standard library module for implementing basic command line applications, authors with more exacting requirements for exactly how their command line applications behave may find it doesn’t provide the necessary level of control. Refer to Choosing an argument parsing library for...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00435] documentation_set: This page contains the API reference information. For a more gentle introduction to Python command-line parsing, have a look at the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00436] documentation_set: The argparse module makes it easy to write user-friendly command-line interfaces. The program defines what arguments it requires, and argparse
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00437] documentation_set: module also automatically generates help and usage messages. The module will also issue errors when users give the program invalid arguments.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00438] documentation_set: The argparse module’s support for command-line interfaces is built around an instance of argparse.ArgumentParser . It is a container for argument specifications and has options that apply to the parser as whole:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00439] documentation_set: The ArgumentParser.add_argument() method attaches individual argument specifications to the parser. It supports positional arguments, options that accept values, and on/off flags:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00440] documentation_set: The ArgumentParser.parse_args() method runs the parser and places the extracted data in a argparse.Namespace object:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00441] documentation_set: If you’re looking for a guide about how to upgrade optparse code to argparse , see Upgrading Optparse Code .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00443] documentation_set: Create a new ArgumentParser object. All parameters should be passed as keyword arguments. Each parameter has its own more detailed description below, but in short they are:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00444] documentation_set: usage - The string describing the program usage (default: generated from arguments added to parser)
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00445] documentation_set: parents - A list of ArgumentParser objects whose arguments should also be included
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00446] documentation_set: prefix_chars - The set of characters that prefix optional arguments (default: ‘-‘)
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00447] documentation_set: fromfile_prefix_chars - The set of characters that prefix files from which additional arguments should be read (default: None )
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00448] documentation_set: conflict_handler - The strategy for resolving conflicting optionals (usually unnecessary)
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00449] documentation_set: allow_abbrev - Allows long options to be abbreviated if the abbreviation is unambiguous (default: True )
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00450] documentation_set: exit_on_error - Determines whether or not ArgumentParser exits with error info when an error occurs. (default: True )
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00451] documentation_set: suggest_on_error - Enables suggestions for mistyped argument choices and subparser names (default: False )
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00452] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.8: In previous versions, allow_abbrev also disabled grouping of short flags such as -vv to mean -v
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00453] documentation_set: By default, ArgumentParser calculates the name of the program to display in help messages depending on the way the Python interpreter was run:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00454] documentation_set: The Python interpreter name followed by sys.argv[0] if a directory or a zipfile was passed as argument.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00455] documentation_set: The Python interpreter name followed by -m followed by the module or package name if the -m option was used.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00456] documentation_set: This default is almost always desirable because it will make the help messages match the string that was used to invoke the program on the command line. However, to change this default behavior, another value can be supplied using the prog= argument to ArgumentParser :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00457] documentation_set: Note that the program name, whether determined from sys.argv[0] , from the __main__ module attributes or from the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00458] documentation_set: prog= argument, is available to help messages using the %(prog)s format specifier.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00459] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.14: The default prog value now reflects how __main__ was actually executed, rather than always being os.path.basename(sys.argv[0]) .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00460] documentation_set: By default, ArgumentParser calculates the usage message from the arguments it contains. The default message can be overridden with the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00461] documentation_set: The %(prog)s format specifier is available to fill in the program name in your usage messages.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00462] documentation_set: When a custom usage message is specified for the main parser, you may also want to consider passing the prog argument to add_subparsers()
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00463] documentation_set: add_parser() , to ensure consistent command prefixes and usage information across subparsers.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00464] documentation_set: description= keyword argument. This argument gives a brief description of what the program does and how it works. In help messages, the description is displayed between the command-line usage string and the help messages for the various arguments.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00465] documentation_set: By default, the description will be line-wrapped so that it fits within the given space. To change this behavior, see the formatter_class argument.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00466] documentation_set: Some programs like to display additional description of the program after the description of the arguments. Such text can be specified using the epilog=
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00467] documentation_set: As with the description argument, the epilog= text is by default line-wrapped, but this behavior can be adjusted with the formatter_class
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00468] documentation_set: Sometimes, several parsers share a common set of arguments. Rather than repeating the definitions of these arguments, a single parser with all the shared arguments and passed to parents= argument to ArgumentParser
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00469] documentation_set: objects, collects all the positional and optional actions from them, and adds these actions to the ArgumentParser object being constructed:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00471] documentation_set: You must fully initialize the parsers before passing them via parents= . If you change the parent parsers after the child parser, those changes will not be reflected in the child.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00472] documentation_set: ArgumentParser objects allow the help formatting to be customized by specifying an alternate formatting class. Currently, there are four such classes:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00473] documentation_set: RawDescriptionHelpFormatter and RawTextHelpFormatter give more control over how textual descriptions are displayed. By default, ArgumentParser objects line-wrap the description and
title: documentation_set

title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00475] documentation_set: RawTextHelpFormatter maintains whitespace for all sorts of help text, including argument descriptions. However, multiple newlines are replaced with one. If you wish to preserve multiple blank lines, add spaces between the newlines.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00476] documentation_set: ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter automatically adds information about default values to each of the argument help messages:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00478] documentation_set: Most command-line options will use - as the prefix, e.g. -f/--foo . Parsers that need to support different or additional prefix characters, e.g. for options like +f or /foo , may specify them using the prefix_chars= argument to the ArgumentParser constructor:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00479] documentation_set: The prefix_chars= argument defaults to '-' . Supplying a set of characters that does not include - will cause -f/--foo options to be disallowed.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00480] documentation_set: Sometimes, when dealing with a particularly long argument list, it may make sense to keep the list of arguments in a file rather than typing it out at the command line. If the fromfile_prefix_chars= argument is given to the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00481] documentation_set: ArgumentParser constructor, then arguments that start with any of the specified characters will be treated as files, and will be replaced by the arguments they contain. For example:

title: documentation_set

text: [D00483] documentation_set: Empty lines are treated as empty strings ( '' ), which are allowed as values but not as arguments. Empty lines that are read as arguments will result in an “unrecognized arguments” error.
title: documentation_set

title: documentation_set

text: [D00490] documentation_set: ArgumentParser objects do not allow two actions with the same option string. By default, ArgumentParser objects raise an exception if an attempt is made to create an argument with an option string that is already in use:

text: [D00517] documentation_set: ArgumentParser objects associate command-line arguments with actions. These actions can do just about anything with the command-line arguments associated with them, though most actions simply add an attribute to the object returned by

Example usag...

text: [D00529] documentation_set: ArgumentParser objects usually associate a single command-line argument with a single action to be taken.
